news_summary: Innovative technologies and community efforts are advancing environmental cleanup, reducing pollution and health risks, generating economic benefits, and enhancing biodiversity conservation.
news_summary_fr: Les technologies innovantes et les initiatives communautaires contribuent à la dépollution de l'environnement, à la réduction de la pollution et des risques sanitaires, à la création de retombées économiques et à la préservation de la biodiversité.
news_summary_es: Las tecnologías innovadoras y las iniciativas comunitarias están impulsando la descontaminación del medio ambiente, reduciendo la contaminación y los riesgos para la salud, generando beneficios económicos y mejorando la conservación de la biodiversidad.

articles.title: Giant fire tornadoes could clean up oil spills faster with less pollution
articles.summary: Researchers have shown that controlled fire whirls can clean up oil spills faster and more cleanly than traditional burning methods. The spinning flames consumed up to 95% of the oil, cut soot emissions by 40%, and could help prevent spills from reaching sensitive marine habitats.
articles.content:
When a major oil spill occurs at sea, emergency crews often face a difficult choice. They can allow the oil to spread across the water, threatening coastlines and marine life, or they can set it on fire.

Burning the oil, a technique known as an in situ burn, can prevent the slick from expanding. However, it also produces thick clouds of black smoke, releases soot into the atmosphere, and leaves behind a layer of unburned residue floating on the ocean's surface.

Now, researchers have demonstrated a striking new approach that could make this process far more effective. In a first-of-its-kind large-scale study, scientists created giant fire whirls, spinning columns of flame that resemble fire tornadoes, and found they burn oil faster and more cleanly than conventional methods.

The rotating vortex draws in large amounts of oxygen, creating a hotter and more efficient flame. As a result, the fire whirl consumed oil more rapidly while producing significantly less pollution.

The study, supported by the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement (BSEE), was led by Dr. Elaine Oran and Dr. Qingsheng Wang of Texas A&M University and Dr. Michael Gollner of the University of California, Berkeley.

"This the first time anyone has conceived using fire whirls for oil spill remediation, and it's really just the beginning," said Oran, professor of aerospace engineering in the College of Engineering. "Our goal is to harness the chaotic nature of fire whirls as a powerful, precise restoration tool, to protect coastlines, marine ecosystems and the environment as a whole."

A Faster, Cleaner Way to Fight Oil Spills

The research introduces an unconventional strategy for dealing with one of the most damaging environmental emergencies.

The devastating Deepwater Horizon disaster in 2010 remains a powerful reminder of the impact offshore oil spills can have. The accident, the largest offshore oil spill in U.S. history, killed 11 workers, claimed the lives of thousands of marine animals, and caused widespread damage to ocean ecosystems.

"We are looking at environmental disasters like oil spills, and identifying ways to remediate them in faster, greener and more sustainable ways," Oran said.

One of the most promising advantages of fire whirls is speed.

According to the researchers, fire whirls can burn crude oil nearly twice as fast as traditional in situ fire pools. Faster removal of oil could give response teams a critical advantage, allowing them to eliminate spills before they spread into sensitive habitats and protected coastal regions.

"Fire whirls burn through crude oil spills nearly twice as fast as in-situ fire pools, potentially giving cleanup crews faster operational and response times to eliminating the oils from spreading," Oran said.

The technique could also reduce one of the biggest drawbacks of burning oil: smoke.

"One of the biggest challenges of burning oil spills is the sheer volume of smoke emitted," Oran said. "Our results show that fire whirls, compared to in-situ fires, dramatically reduce overall emissions."

Acting like a giant incinerator, the spinning flames destroy many of the particles responsible for dense smoke plumes. The process also vaporizes most of the oil before it can remain behind as a toxic tar-like residue on the water.

The findings may have applications beyond oil spill response. A better understanding of how fire whirls form and behave could help engineers develop more efficient combustion systems and improve efforts to predict and manage wildfires.

"Our study has universal applications," Oran said. "By understanding the physical laws that govern fire whirls, we can harness their power beyond oil spill remediation."

Building a 17-Foot-Tall Fire Whirl

Most previous studies of fire whirls have been conducted on a much smaller scale in laboratory settings.

To explore whether the phenomenon could be useful for real-world oil spill cleanup, the research team designed an experiment large enough to mimic more realistic conditions.

"The scale of our experiment is one of the reasons why our investigation is so unique, and what sets it apart as a first-of-its-kind," Oran said.

The researchers built a 16-foot-tall triangular structure with three walls that allowed them to carefully control airflow. At the center, they placed a 1.5-meter-wide pool of crude oil floating on water.

Once ignited at the Texas A&M Engineering Extension Service (TEEX) Brayton Fire Training Field, the setup generated a powerful fire whirl that reached nearly 17 feet in height.

The results, published in Fuel, showed major improvements over conventional oil burning techniques.

"The fire whirls burned the oil about 40 percent faster, cut soot emissions by 40 percent, and achieved up to 95 percent fuel consumption efficiency compared to in-situ fire tests," Oran said.

Finding the Fire Whirl "Goldilocks" Zone

Despite their impressive performance, fire whirls are not easy to control.

"Fire whirls are incredibly powerful, and can be incredibly beneficial," Oran said. "But they're also sensitive and only reach high efficiency when the conditions are just right."

Strong winds can destabilize the spinning column or cause it to collapse altogether. Insufficient airflow control can prevent the vortex from forming, causing the fire to behave more like a conventional burn.

Researchers also discovered that the thickness of the oil layer plays an important role. When the slick became too deep, the fire whirls went out before consuming all of the fuel.

This narrow range of ideal conditions, described by the researchers as a "Goldilocks" zone, highlights both the promise and the challenge of bringing the technology into practical use.

Fire Tornadoes as Future Cleanup Tools

The team envisions a future in which portable systems could be deployed directly over burning oil spills to intentionally generate fire whirls on demand.

If successful, such systems could transform emergency oil spill response by converting ordinary fires into highly efficient cleanup tools.

"This study is more than just an experiment, it's a glimpse into a future where fire isn't a force of destruction, but a tool to protect our oceans and planet," Oran said.

For now, the research stands as an impressive demonstration of what can happen when scientists rethink a familiar natural phenomenon.

It suggests that even one of nature's most intimidating forces can potentially be redirected to address some of the world's most urgent environmental challenges.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers developed a new method using controlled fire whirls to clean up oil spills faster and with less pollution. This innovation has broad environmental benefits by potentially improving emergency response to oil spills, reducing harmful emissions, and protecting marine ecosystems. The study is detailed, focuses on a single impactful topic, and offers hope for more sustainable environmental disaster management.
articles.personality_title: Fire tornadoes offer faster, cleaner way to clean up oil spills
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When oil spills happen at sea, burning the oil is one way to stop it from spreading. But this method creates thick smoke and leaves harmful leftovers in the water.

**What happened** – Scientists from Texas A&M University and the University of California, Berkeley, tested giant fire whirls—spinning columns of flame like fire tornadoes—to burn oil spills. In a large experiment, they created a 17-foot-tall fire whirl that burned oil up to 40% faster, used 95% of the oil, and produced 40% less soot than regular burning.

**Impact** – This new method could help clean oil spills more quickly and with less pollution, protecting marine life and coastlines. The spinning flames use more oxygen, making the fire hotter and cleaner. It also reduces the thick smoke and sticky oil residue that usually harm the environment.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to develop portable devices that can create fire whirls on demand during real oil spills. They also plan to study how to control these fires better since they only work perfectly under certain conditions like the right wind and oil thickness.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Controlled fire tornadoes could become a faster, cleaner tool to fight oil spills and protect the ocean.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les tornades de feu offrent un moyen plus rapide et plus propre de nettoyer les marées noires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lorsqu'une marée noire se produit en mer, brûler le pétrole est une méthode pour empêcher sa propagation. Mais cette méthode crée une fumée épaisse et laisse des résidus nocifs dans l'eau.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l'université Texas A&M et de l'université de Californie à Berkeley ont testé de gigantesques tourbillons de feu, sortes de tornades de flammes, pour brûler les marées noires. Dans une grande expérience, ils ont créé un tourbillon de feu de 5 mètres de haut qui a brûlé le pétrole jusqu'à 40 % plus vite, consommé 95 % du pétrole et produit 40 % moins de suie que la combustion classique.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode pourrait aider à nettoyer les marées noires plus rapidement et avec moins de pollution, protégeant ainsi la vie marine et les côtes. Les flammes tournantes utilisent plus d'oxygène, rendant le feu plus chaud et plus propre. Cela réduit aussi la fumée épaisse et les résidus collants qui nuisent à l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent développer des appareils portables capables de créer ces tourbillons de feu à la demande lors de vraies marées noires. Ils prévoient aussi d’étudier comment mieux contrôler ces feux, car ils fonctionnent parfaitement seulement dans certaines conditions, comme le bon vent et l’épaisseur du pétrole.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les tornades de feu contrôlées pourraient devenir un outil plus rapide et plus propre pour lutter contre les marées noires et protéger l’océan.
articles.personality_title_es: Los tornados de fuego ofrecen una forma más rápida y limpia de limpiar derrames de petróleo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando ocurre un derrame de petróleo en el mar, quemar el petróleo es una forma de evitar que se extienda. Pero esta técnica crea mucho humo y deja residuos dañinos en el agua.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Texas A&M y la Universidad de California en Berkeley probaron remolinos de fuego gigantes, columnas giratorias de llama parecidas a tornados de fuego, para quemar los derrames de petróleo. En un gran experimento, crearon un remolino de fuego de casi 5 metros de altura que quemó el petróleo hasta un 40 % más rápido, consumió el 95 % del petróleo y produjo un 40 % menos de hollín que los métodos tradicionales.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método podría ayudar a limpiar derrames de petróleo más rápido y con menos contaminación, protegiendo la vida marina y las costas. Las llamas giratorias usan más oxígeno, haciendo el fuego más caliente y limpio. También reduce el humo espeso y los residuos pegajosos que dañan el medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren crear dispositivos portátiles que puedan generar estos remolinos de fuego cuando haya derrames reales. También estudiarán cómo controlar mejor estos fuegos, ya que funcionan bien solo bajo ciertas condiciones como el viento adecuado y el grosor del petróleo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los tornados de fuego controlados podrían ser una herramienta más rápida y limpia para combatir derrames de petróleo y proteger el océano.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a towering, swirling fire whirl rising above a calm ocean surface dotted with floating oil patches, its vibrant orange and red flames forming a dynamic spiral that consumes the dark slick below, surrounded by soft, warm hues of sky and water, symbolizing a powerful yet controlled natural force cleansing the sea with minimal smoke and pollution.

articles.title: Electric vehicles cut pollution in China – and prevent 260,000 premature deaths
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01781-5Having fewer fossil-fuel powered cars on the road is reducing some pollutants, but not others.
articles.content:
Credit: David Paul Morris/Bloomberg via Getty

Across China, a marked rise in people driving electric vehicles has led to a drop in air pollution in many cities. As a result, fewer people are dying from pollution-related diseases.

Outdoor air pollution causes more than four million premature deaths annually, and about one-quarter of them occur in China. Vehicles powered by fossil fuels are a main contributor to that pollution, releasing carbon monoxide, particulate matter, nitrogen oxides and other compounds that are dangerous to humans when inhaled.

EVs started growing in popularity about a decade ago. Around this time, researchers began studying the connection between vehicle electrification and air quality. Although modelling studies projected that EVs would lead to drastic reductions in pollutants from fossil-fuelled cars, they lacked empirical evidence to prove it. Now, large studies in countries including China, and residential areas across the state of California, have measured lower levels of pollutants in the air as more EVs are added to the roads.

China is the world leader in the manufacture and sale of EVs. In the past two decades, it is estimated that the Chinese government has invested hundreds of billions of US dollars into subsidies, tax incentives and other programmes to boost the production and sale of EVs. More than half of all cars sold in China in 2025 were electric.

This has led to a large drop in certain pollutants, according to a study published last month that used satellite data to measure air quality1. Across 150 cities, with an uptake of ‘new energy vehicles’ — which include battery-electric, hybrid and hydrogen-powered cars — carbon monoxide levels are down by more than 30% than an alternate, hypothetical scenario in which all vehicles on the roads of these cities used traditional internal combustion engines. And the amount of particulate matter with a diameter of less than 2.5 micrometres has decreased by more than 23%.

The authors estimate that improved outdoor air quality in these cities prevented 262,000 premature deaths. Long-term exposure to pollutant can cause strokes, heart disease, lung cancer, respiratory diseases and infections.

“The results are both encouraging and sobering,” says Qiangqiang Yuan, a remote-sensing researcher at Wuhan University in China and co-author of the study.

Cleaner California

The public health benefits of fewer gas guzzlers and more fully or partially electric cars on the roads extend beyond China. Throughout California, the rapid adoption of ‘zero-emissions vehicles’ led to improved air quality through reduced nitrogen dioxide concentrations, according to a 2026 study2. The authors analysed daily measurements of nitrogen dioxide in each of roughly 1,700 residential areas from 2019 to 2023. They compared the trends in pollution levels with the number of of EV registrations, finding that for every 200 new zero-emission-vehicle registrations in an area, nitrogen dioxide levels fell by 1.1%. Some places reduced nitrogen dioxide pollution by around 4%.

The China study found that nitrogen oxide concentrations decreased modestly, by 7.92% compared with the non-EV scenario. Enrico Ferrero, an atmospheric physicist at the University of Eastern Piedmont in Vercelli, Italy, thinks this is because nitrogen oxides are uniquely complex chemical products. Unlike carbon monoxide and particulate matter, nitrogen dioxide forms after one of its components, nitric oxide, is emitted from a car’s exhaust and mixes with ozone, a harmful compound already in the atmosphere. Reducing fuel-powered road vehicles, then, is only one piece of the nitrogen oxide emissions puzzle.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant real-world impact where the rise of electric vehicles in China has led to measurable reductions in harmful air pollutants, preventing an estimated 260,000 premature deaths. The scope is broad, affecting millions of people in China and also showing benefits in California, with detailed empirical evidence supporting the environmental and public health benefits of EV adoption.
articles.personality_title: Electric vehicles in China cut pollution and save lives
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Air pollution is a major health problem worldwide, causing millions of early deaths each year. Cars that run on fossil fuels release harmful gases and tiny particles into the air, which people breathe in. China, with a large number of vehicles, has been working to reduce this pollution.

**What happened** – Over the past decade, more people in China have started driving electric vehicles (EVs), which do not burn fossil fuels. A recent study looked at air quality in 150 Chinese cities and found that pollution from carbon monoxide and tiny particles dropped significantly as more EVs appeared on the roads. This change has helped prevent about 260,000 early deaths caused by air pollution.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows real proof that switching to electric cars can make the air cleaner and protect people’s health. The study used satellite data to measure pollution and compared what actually happened to a scenario where all cars stayed fuel-powered. The results also match findings from California, where more zero-emission cars have reduced nitrogen dioxide pollution in many neighborhoods.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to study how different types of pollution respond to more electric vehicles. Since some pollutants, like nitrogen oxides, form in complex ways, other solutions may be needed alongside EVs to further improve air quality. Governments might also keep encouraging EV use with policies and investments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The rise of electric vehicles in China has led to cleaner air and saved hundreds of thousands of lives by cutting harmful pollution.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les véhicules électriques en Chine réduisent la pollution et sauvent des vies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La pollution de l’air est un grave problème de santé dans le monde, causant des millions de décès prématurés chaque année. Les voitures à carburant fossile rejettent des gaz nocifs et de fines particules dans l’air, que les gens respirent. La Chine, avec un grand nombre de véhicules, cherche à réduire cette pollution.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Au cours de la dernière décennie, de plus en plus de personnes en Chine ont commencé à conduire des véhicules électriques (VE), qui ne brûlent pas de carburant fossile. Une étude récente a examiné la qualité de l’air dans 150 villes chinoises et a constaté une forte baisse de la pollution au monoxyde de carbone et aux particules fines à mesure que les VE se multipliaient. Ce changement a permis d’éviter environ 260 000 décès prématurés liés à la pollution.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre une preuve réelle que passer aux voitures électriques peut rendre l’air plus propre et protéger la santé des gens. L’étude a utilisé des données satellites pour mesurer la pollution et a comparé la réalité à un scénario où toutes les voitures seraient restées à carburant fossile. Ces résultats correspondent aussi à ceux de la Californie, où plus de voitures zéro émission ont réduit la pollution par le dioxyde d’azote dans plusieurs quartiers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier comment différents types de pollution réagissent à l’augmentation des véhicules électriques. Comme certains polluants, comme les oxydes d’azote, se forment de manière complexe, d’autres solutions pourraient être nécessaires en plus des VE pour améliorer encore la qualité de l’air. Les gouvernements pourraient aussi continuer à encourager l’usage des VE avec des politiques et des investissements.

**En une phrase** – L’essor des véhicules électriques en Chine a rendu l’air plus propre et sauvé des centaines de milliers de vies en réduisant la pollution nocive.
articles.personality_title_es: Vehículos eléctricos en China reducen la contaminación y salvan vidas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La contaminación del aire es un grave problema de salud en todo el mundo, causando millones de muertes prematuras cada año. Los autos que usan combustibles fósiles emiten gases dañinos y partículas pequeñas al aire que las personas respiran. China, con muchos vehículos, ha estado trabajando para reducir esta contaminación.

**Qué pasó** – En la última década, más personas en China comenzaron a usar vehículos eléctricos (VE), que no queman combustibles fósiles. Un estudio reciente analizó la calidad del aire en 150 ciudades chinas y encontró que la contaminación por monóxido de carbono y partículas pequeñas bajó mucho a medida que hubo más VE en las calles. Esto ayudó a evitar unas 260,000 muertes tempranas causadas por la contaminación.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra evidencia real de que cambiar a autos eléctricos puede limpiar el aire y proteger la salud. El estudio usó datos de satélites para medir la contaminación y comparó lo que pasó con un escenario donde todos los autos seguían usando combustibles fósiles. Los resultados también coinciden con los de California, donde más autos sin emisiones redujeron la contaminación por dióxido de nitrógeno en varios vecindarios.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cómo diferentes tipos de contaminación responden a más vehículos eléctricos. Como algunos contaminantes, como los óxidos de nitrógeno, se forman de manera compleja, podrían necesitarse otras soluciones además de los VE para mejorar aún más la calidad del aire. Los gobiernos también podrían seguir apoyando el uso de VE con políticas e inversiones.

**Resumen en una frase** – El aumento de vehículos eléctricos en China ha limpiado el aire y salvado cientos de miles de vidas al reducir la contaminación dañina.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant cityscape with numerous sleek electric vehicles gliding silently along clean streets beneath a clear sky, while stylized satellite orbits and soft glowing data lines above symbolize improved air quality and reduced pollution, all rendered in warm, natural tones with a detailed, painterly style.

articles.title: Your local park is bringing in the green (and by that, we mean money)
articles.content:
In an increasingly divided nation, Americans agree on at least two things. For one, politicians across the political spectrum are scrambling to get more housing built, which happens to be an accidentally powerful way to fight climate change. And two, Americans love their parks: A recent poll found that 88 percent of them visited one in the past year. Nearly 90 percent of people who voted for Kamala Harris, and 80 percent of those who voted for Donald Trump, consider these spaces critical infrastructure in their communities.

That alone should encourage elected officials to build as many of them as possible. But a new report finds another, potentially even more motivating, factor for American cities: For every dollar invested in parks and recreation, communities reap $3 in local economic benefits each year. “You really do get so much goodness out of them,” said Will Klein, director of parks research at the Trust for Public Land, which produced the report. “People are healthier, people connect with each other. They drive business activity, especially for small businesses.”

Parks aren’t as much about land as they are about people. In an increasingly commodified world, they’re one of the few remaining public places where folks can roam without the pressure of spending money. That makes them a critical kind of “third place,” somewhere to gather beyond the workplace and the home. Whereas people must pay a premium to use a gym, they can use a park or rec center for free.

This brings huge benefits, and cost savings, to public health. The new report notes that the United States spends $5.3 trillion annually on health care. Physical inactivity, which greatly increases the risk of chronic problems like cardiovascular disease, costs the country more than $200 billion a year. “Our polling this year showed that the most popular place in America in 2025 to run around and play and exercise are parks and public spaces, much more popular than private gyms,” Klein said. “That physical activity has real health and economic benefits, about $2,000 per person in health care savings each year.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Parks boost mental health as well. Simply being among greenery boosts positive well-being, research has shown. Parks also foster social interaction and reduce loneliness, which is a public health crisis of its own. This kind of commerce-free third place is especially important for the elderly, who may be living on fixed incomes and can’t afford to frequent cafes and the like. “There’s movie nights in the park, concerts in the park,” Klein said. “Just playing on the playground, talking to neighbors, having barbecues — all that stuff allows people to afford that higher quality of life.”

Even though they exist outside of the frenzy of capitalism, parks provide major economic value. The crowds they attract, for example, filter into surrounding neighborhoods, buying food and drinks for picnics or perusing mom-and-pop shops and boutiques. Famous green spaces — New York City’s Central Park, Chicago’s Millennium Park, San Francisco’s Golden Gate Park and newly minted Sunset Dunes — attract tourists, too. The Trust for Public Land says that the Florida Gulf Coast Trail, the 420-mile greenway it’s helping develop, will bring $200 million in economic activity in Sarasota County alone by attracting bicyclists and other recreationists.

Even if you own a home near a park but never visit it, you’re benefiting economically in a way. “People want to live near green spaces,” Klein said. “So you see increased property values, which supports a broader tax base, which can be reinvested into community benefits through the increased property tax revenue.”

The trick is ensuring everyone — not just those who can afford condos and single-family homes — can enjoy the aura of these jewels. While new housing developments might seem at odds with green spaces, the two can exist in harmony. Even if they’re crammed into the densest of cities, affordable complexes can incorporate pocket gardens, which have the added benefit of reducing increasingly unbearable urban temperatures. Some developers are even building communities around working farms, known as agrihoods, which bring yet another benefit of local food production.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Any additional green space will also help cities adapt to one of the stranger consequences of climate change: It’s raining a lot harder. City sewer systems were designed to handle the rainstorms of old, but are overwhelmed by the additional water falling today. By soaking up some of that liquid, parks help save money in two ways: They reduce the amount of water that a city has to pay to manage, and they help prevent the surrounding neighborhood from flooding, avoiding property damage.

More so than ever before, then, the humble green space is a surprisingly powerful way to solve a bunch of problems at once — improving mental health, helping cities adapt to climate change, and supercharging economic activity. “Parks,” Klein said, “are actually one of these solutions hiding under the feet of all these local leaders.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the broad and significant positive impact of parks on public health, local economies, social well-being, and climate adaptation. It provides detailed evidence of measurable benefits such as economic returns on investment, health care savings, mental health improvements, and urban climate resilience. The story focuses on a single, meaningful topic with substantial context, demonstrating how parks serve as critical infrastructure with wide societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: Parks bring big benefits to communities and local economies
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Americans strongly support parks, with nearly 9 out of 10 people visiting one last year. Parks are seen as important community places by people from different political backgrounds, showing their wide appeal.

**What happened** – A new report from the Trust for Public Land shows that every dollar spent on parks brings about three dollars back to local economies each year. Parks encourage people to be active, connect with neighbors, and support small businesses. They also help cities handle heavy rain by absorbing water and preventing floods.

**Impact** – Parks improve physical and mental health by offering free spaces to exercise and relax among nature. This lowers health care costs by around $2,000 per person annually. They also increase nearby property values and attract tourists, adding money to communities. Parks serve as “third places” where people gather outside home and work without needing to spend money.

**What's next step** – Cities can invest more in parks while building affordable housing nearby to make green spaces accessible to everyone. Combining parks with new housing and community farms can improve quality of life and help cities adapt to climate change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Investing in parks creates healthier communities, boosts local economies, and helps cities manage climate challenges all at once.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les parcs apportent de grands bénéfices aux communautés et à l'économie locale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Près de 9 Américains sur 10 ont visité un parc l'année dernière, et ces espaces verts sont considérés comme essentiels dans les communautés, quel que soit le camp politique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un nouveau rapport du Trust for Public Land montre que chaque dollar investi dans les parcs rapporte environ trois dollars à l'économie locale chaque année. Les parcs encouragent l'activité physique, les liens entre voisins et soutiennent les petites entreprises. Ils aident aussi les villes à gérer les fortes pluies en absorbant l'eau et en évitant les inondations.

**Impact** – Les parcs améliorent la santé physique et mentale en offrant des espaces gratuits pour se déplacer et se détendre dans la nature. Cela réduit les coûts de santé d'environ 2 000 dollars par personne par an. Ils augmentent la valeur des propriétés voisines et attirent les touristes, ce qui apporte de l'argent aux communautés. Les parcs sont des « tiers lieux » où les gens se retrouvent en dehors de la maison et du travail sans avoir besoin de dépenser.

**Prochaine étape** – Les villes peuvent investir davantage dans les parcs tout en construisant des logements abordables à proximité pour rendre ces espaces accessibles à tous. Associer parcs, logements et fermes communautaires peut améliorer la qualité de vie et aider les villes à s’adapter au changement climatique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Investir dans les parcs crée des communautés plus saines, dynamise l’économie locale et aide les villes à faire face aux défis climatiques simultanément.
articles.personality_title_es: Los parques aportan grandes beneficios a las comunidades y economías locales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Casi 9 de cada 10 estadounidenses visitaron un parque el año pasado, y estos espacios verdes son vistos como esenciales en las comunidades, sin importar la afiliación política.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo informe del Trust for Public Land muestra que cada dólar invertido en parques genera unos tres dólares en beneficios económicos locales cada año. Los parques fomentan la actividad física, las conexiones entre vecinos y apoyan a los pequeños negocios. También ayudan a las ciudades a manejar las lluvias intensas al absorber agua y prevenir inundaciones.

**Impacto** – Los parques mejoran la salud física y mental al ofrecer espacios gratuitos para moverse y relajarse en la naturaleza. Esto reduce los costos de salud en alrededor de 2,000 dólares por persona al año. Además, aumentan el valor de las propiedades cercanas y atraen turistas, lo que aporta dinero a las comunidades. Los parques son “terceros lugares” donde la gente se reúne fuera del hogar y el trabajo sin tener que gastar dinero.

**Próximo paso** – Las ciudades pueden invertir más en parques mientras construyen viviendas asequibles cerca para que todos puedan acceder a estos espacios. Combinar parques con nuevas viviendas y granjas comunitarias puede mejorar la calidad de vida y ayudar a las ciudades a adaptarse al cambio climático.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Invertir en parques crea comunidades más saludables, impulsa las economías locales y ayuda a las ciudades a enfrentar los retos climáticos al mismo tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant community park scene at golden hour, featuring interconnected silhouettes of diverse people enjoying playgrounds, picnics, and open green spaces that seamlessly blend into nearby small businesses and cozy affordable housing with rooftop gardens, all surrounded by gentle rain showers being absorbed by lush pocket gardens and urban farms, symbolizing health, economic growth, social connection, and climate resilience.

articles.title: In the Smoky Mountains, a volunteer effort aims to document every species — before it’s too late
articles.content:
A gentle shower fell as four people in rain gear made their way deep into a spruce-fir forest high in Great Smoky Mountains National Park. Ducking beneath bright green underbrush and stepping away from the road, a hush took over.

Just a few steps in, they came across an aging yellow birch tree covered in moss.

But it wasn’t just moss. James Hollinger, a retired computer scientist turned amateur lichen scientist, leaned closer and spotted a rare, spongy lichen that has been documented about a dozen times in the park. As far as he knows, it does not appear in any botanical guidebooks.

“So, we could, right here right now, come up with a common name for it,” Hollinger said excitedly, as fellow volunteer and lichenologist Laura Boggess unfolded her magnifying lens. Counting carefully, she found more than 17 other moss and lichen species on just one side of the tree.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Every square foot of the Smokies teems with life that most visitors never notice: lichens clinging to bark, fungi hidden in fallen logs, and salamanders darting beneath damp leaves. Scientists and volunteers say paying attention to those small creatures — and returning often enough to notice when they change — has grown increasingly urgent as climate change alters the park’s ecosystems and federal agencies see deep cuts that threaten long-term monitoring and biodiversity research.

Hollinger, Boggess, and the others in the group call themselves the Gang of Retirees in Search of Life’s Diversity, or “GRISLD.” Not all are retired — Boggess is beginning a teaching job at Warren Wilson College in the fall — but they share a habit of spending hours moving deliberately through remote corners of the park, documenting species few people will ever see. Connected through a listserv and their keen interest in the Smokies’ rich biodiversity, the group quietly contributes to a long-running project called the all taxa biodiversity inventory, or ATBI, conducted in partnership with the park.

The Great Smoky Mountains are the most biodiverse site in the national park system. Every square foot of the park teems with life, much of which park visitors rarely see. Katie Myers / Grist

“We’ll hike into these places that other researchers don’t have the resources, the funding to,” Hollinger said. “We watch all these things and keep an eye on how things are changing.”

The Smokies project is one of the oldest and longest-running all taxa biodiversity inventories in the country, one of several decades-long efforts to document biodiversity in dozens of ecological hotspots around the world. That work has taken on increasing urgency in the Great Smoky Mountains, the most biodiverse site in the national park system and a global hotspot for salamanders, fungi, mosses, and other less-studied forms of life.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The mountains’ varied elevations and countless microclimates may help some species survive a warming world by providing pockets of cooler habitat. But climate change is also reshaping the park in visible ways, from an increase in invasive insects and dying trees to more frequent floods, fires, and violent storms. The inventory is conducted with the park and managed by the nonprofit Discover Life in America, where Will Kuhn — one of the hikers threading through the wet forest that morning — leads scientific research.

“We’re up to over 22,000 species of everything that has been documented here in the Smokies,” Kuhn said. More than 1,000 of them documented since 1998 are new to science, a number believed to just scratch the surface. “That is maybe a third to a quarter of the actual diversity here.”

Finding a new species might seem like a rare joy, but it happens regularly, Kuhn says. Larger, charismatic species are well documented, but little ones, such as mites, mosses, and microscopic plankton-like rotifers are often understudied.

Much of the park’s biodiversity data is collected during spring and summer, when academic researchers tend to visit, Kuhn said. Volunteers are there year-round, however, tracking species that are active in colder months or, like many birds, pass through while migrating. “The park’s really known during that time of the year, but what about the things that are off-period?” Hollinger said, turning over a log as a red-cheeked salamander scampered into the wet leaves.

A red-cheeked salamander scampers under a log. Volunteers take photos of every species they log and upload them to iNaturalist, a citizen science database. Courtesy of Will Kuhn

Although the Park Service grants permits to academic researchers, its relationships with local nonprofits and tourism-dependent communities allow it to support ecological work it cannot manage on its own. Those organizations can also raise money in times of need, in one recent case helping to keep the park open while salaries were on pause during the 2025 government shutdown.

“Ultimately, we’re able to spend money on things that benefit the park but that a federal agency just can’t do,” Kuhn said.

Retired biologist Paul Super coordinated research in the park for over two decades. He’s interested in lichens, mosses, insects, and other small creatures in part because of the way they hold moisture, keeping the mountain cool and foggy. If they die, the water cycle will change.

“Regulating the moisture in these high elevation areas is pretty important because we’re at the top of the watershed, and everybody’s drinking water is downhill from here,” Super said.

In the decades he’s spent in the park, he’s seen long-term changes unfold. Warming temperatures are rippling through the food chain, making way for invasive parasites like the woolly adelgid. The tiny insect, which is native to Asia, has infested and killed thousands of the park’s hemlocks, a towering tree sometimes called the “redwood of the East.” Other pests have attacked Fraser firs, elms, and white and green ash trees that keep streams cool for temperature-sensitive aquatic species like the beloved brook trout.

The high-elevation ecosystems of the Smokies are “sky islands” – isolated pockets of unique species that depend upon cooler, wetter conditions. When the climate warms, there’s nowhere else to go. Some may disappear before anyone even knows they’re there.

To Super, logging these species is about noticing the minute, day-to-day, month-to-month, year-to-year changes that become earth-shattering over time. “The visitor coming here for a day or a week is not going to notice things and know that this is not what it used to be,” he said.

Laura Boggess was born and raised in western North Carolina and drawn to science through a lifetime love of climbing the region’s remote cliffs. She considers these data-gathering trips a critical way to monitor the changing climate from the ground up. “The small ways, the paying attention, the naming of a species, which isn’t a small thing, but it’s like an accumulation of small, cooperative creation,” she said. “It is even more important as we enter into even more rapid change.”

There is so much to see in the park that it took the volunteers about two hours to go half a mile. Even as they left the trail and returned to the road, they found a rare parasitic fungus. The magnifying glass came out, and everyone slowly leaned in for a good look.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant volunteer-driven biodiversity inventory effort in the Great Smoky Mountains National Park, which is the most biodiverse site in the US national park system. This long-term scientific and community initiative documents thousands of species, including many new to science, helping to monitor and potentially mitigate the impacts of climate change on a critical ecosystem. The story highlights meaningful environmental conservation work with broad ecological and societal benefits, supported by detailed context and examples.
articles.personality_title: Volunteers document thousands of species in Great Smoky Mountains to track climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Great Smoky Mountains National Park is the most biologically diverse national park in the United States. It is home to thousands of species, many of which are small and hard to see, like lichens, mosses, and salamanders. Climate change is affecting the park’s environment, making it important to keep track of these species closely.

**What happened** – A group of volunteers, called the Gang of Retirees in Search of Life’s Diversity (GRISLD), spends time exploring remote parts of the park to find and record different species. They work alongside scientists and the nonprofit Discover Life in America. Together, they have documented over 22,000 species, including more than 1,000 new to science since 1998. Volunteers use tools like magnifying lenses and take photos to help identify and track these species.

**Impact** – This effort is important because many species in the park are small and easily overlooked, yet they play key roles in the ecosystem, like keeping the mountain moist and cool. By documenting species regularly, volunteers help scientists understand how climate change is affecting the park. Some species live only in cool, high places called "sky islands" and could disappear as the climate warms. The data collected also supports efforts to protect the park’s water and wildlife.

**What's next step** – The volunteers and scientists will continue to explore different parts of the park year-round, including colder months when fewer researchers visit. They plan to keep adding new species to the inventory and monitor changes over time. This ongoing work will help guide conservation efforts and inform how the park responds to environmental threats.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Volunteers and scientists working together in the Great Smoky Mountains are discovering and tracking thousands of species to better understand and protect this unique ecosystem as it faces climate change.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des bénévoles recensent des milliers d’espèces dans les Great Smoky Mountains pour suivre le changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le parc national des Great Smoky Mountains est le parc national le plus riche en biodiversité des États-Unis. Il abrite des milliers d’espèces, souvent petites et difficiles à voir, comme les lichens, les mousses et les salamandres. Le changement climatique modifie l’environnement du parc, rendant essentiel le suivi de ces espèces.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un groupe de bénévoles, appelé le Gang of Retirees in Search of Life’s Diversity (GRISLD), explore les zones reculées du parc pour trouver et enregistrer différentes espèces. Ils travaillent avec des scientifiques et l’association Discover Life in America. Ensemble, ils ont recensé plus de 22 000 espèces, dont plus de 1 000 nouvelles pour la science depuis 1998. Les bénévoles utilisent des loupes et prennent des photos pour aider à identifier et suivre ces espèces.

**Impact** – Cet effort est important car beaucoup d’espèces du parc sont petites et souvent ignorées, mais elles jouent un rôle clé dans l’écosystème, par exemple en maintenant l’humidité et la fraîcheur des montagnes. En documentant régulièrement les espèces, les bénévoles aident les scientifiques à comprendre comment le changement climatique affecte le parc. Certaines espèces vivent uniquement dans des zones fraîches et isolées appelées « îles célestes » et pourraient disparaître avec le réchauffement. Les données collectées soutiennent aussi la protection de l’eau et de la faune du parc.

**Prochaine étape** – Les bénévoles et scientifiques continueront d’explorer le parc toute l’année, y compris en hiver, quand il y a moins de chercheurs. Ils prévoient d’ajouter de nouvelles espèces à l’inventaire et de suivre les changements avec le temps. Ce travail continu aidera à mieux protéger le parc face aux menaces environnementales.

**Phrase clé** – Des bénévoles et des scientifiques travaillent ensemble dans les Great Smoky Mountains pour découvrir et suivre des milliers d’espèces afin de mieux comprendre et protéger cet écosystème unique face au changement climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Voluntarios documentan miles de especies en Great Smoky Mountains para monitorear el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Parque Nacional Great Smoky Mountains es el parque nacional más biodiverso de Estados Unidos. Alberga miles de especies, muchas pequeñas y difíciles de ver, como líquenes, musgos y salamandras. El cambio climático está afectando el ambiente del parque, por lo que es importante vigilar estas especies.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de voluntarios llamado Gang of Retirees in Search of Life’s Diversity (GRISLD) explora zonas remotas del parque para encontrar y registrar diferentes especies. Trabajan junto a científicos y la organización Discover Life in America. Han documentado más de 22,000 especies, incluyendo más de 1,000 nuevas para la ciencia desde 1998. Los voluntarios usan lentes de aumento y toman fotos para ayudar a identificar y seguir estas especies.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo es importante porque muchas especies pequeñas son clave para el ecosistema, como mantener la humedad y frescura de la montaña. Al documentar las especies regularmente, los voluntarios ayudan a los científicos a entender cómo afecta el cambio climático al parque. Algunas especies viven solo en lugares frescos y aislados llamados "islas del cielo" y podrían desaparecer con el calentamiento. Los datos también apoyan la conservación del agua y la vida silvestre.

**Próximo paso** – Los voluntarios y científicos seguirán explorando el parque todo el año, incluso en invierno cuando hay menos investigadores. Planean añadir nuevas especies al inventario y monitorear cambios con el tiempo. Este trabajo ayudará a proteger mejor el parque frente a amenazas ambientales.

**Frase clave** – Voluntarios y científicos juntos en Great Smoky Mountains están descubriendo y monitoreando miles de especies para entender y proteger este ecosistema único ante el cambio climático.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a moss-covered yellow birch tree in a misty spruce-fir forest, with delicate, spongy lichens and various small mosses vividly textured on its bark, surrounded by abstract silhouettes of hikers in rain gear carefully observing and documenting the rich, hidden biodiversity, with a subtle glow highlighting a red-cheeked salamander darting beneath damp leaves and a rare parasitic fungus on a fallen log nearby.
